data.title: Lyft targets teen market with enhanced security ridesharing parental oversight
data.description:
Lyft (LYFT) is developing Lyft Teen, a new ridesharing service designed to offer enhanced safety for teenage riders and greater oversight for parents by pairing teens with highly rated drivers.

The service mirrors a similar offering from Uber (UBER), allowing parents to create and manage accounts for children ages 13 to 17, who can request rides or meal deliveries on demand. Parents receive real-time trip and delivery tracking, while teens are matched exclusively with top-rated drivers.

With Lyft Teen, the company is reversing its prior policy that barred unaccompanied riders ages 13 to 17 from requesting rides. To address parental safety concerns and expand its total addressable market ((TAM)), Lyft will introduce additional safeguards, including PIN authentication, automatic audio recording, and parent-to-driver communication tools.

According to a post from Lyft CEO David Risher on X,  Lyft Teen is expected to launch in early 2026 across hundreds of U.S. cities.

data.title: HCA outlines $76.5B–$80B 2026 revenue guidance amid expanded resiliency initiatives and $10B share buyback
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: HCA Healthcare, Inc. (HCA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Samuel Hazen described the quarter as closing out a year of “strong results that were mostly consistent with the previous quarters in 2025,” highlighting the 19th straight quarter of volume growth and the impact of network investments, capacity management, and improved quality outcomes. He noted, “Revenue increased 6.7% compared to the prior year quarter and with disciplined expense management, margins improved both sequentially and year-over-year.”
  • Hazen emphasized a record 47 million patient encounters for the year, noting significant investments in network expansion, workforce development, and clinical capabilities.
  • He addressed evolving policy matters, including “the expired enhanced premium tax credits, the ongoing developments related to Medicaid supplemental payment programs and the Rural Health Transformation program,” stating, “We believe our core business remains strong with forecasted volumes in our long-term 2% to 3% growth range.”
  • On organizational resilience, Hazen detailed enhancements in operating imperatives, management systems, and leadership development, alongside increased hospital capacity and outpatient facilities.
  • CFO Mike Marks stated, "We were pleased with the results of the fourth quarter of 2025, which reflected strong operational performance combined with disciplined capital allocation." He reported admissions up 2.4%, equivalent admissions up 2.5%, and shared that “the 80 basis point improvement in adjusted EBITDA margin in the quarter was driven primarily by solid revenue growth, good results in labor management and improvements in other operating expenses.”
  • Marks highlighted a retro payment from Virginia impacting supplemental payments, robust cash flow from operations at $2.4 billion for the quarter, and share repurchases totaling $2.6 billion in Q4.

Outlook

  • Marks guided for 2026 revenues between $76.5 billion and $80 billion, adjusted EBITDA between $15.55 billion and $16.45 billion, net income attributable to HCA Healthcare between $6.5 billion and $7 billion, and diluted EPS between $29.10 and $31.50. Capital spending is expected to increase to a range of $5 billion to $5.5 billion.
  • The 2026 outlook assumes “growth in equivalent admissions between 2% and 3%,” an adverse adjusted EBITDA impact of $600 million to $900 million from health insurance exchanges, with an offset of approximately $400 million from resiliency initiatives.
  • Marks stated, “We expect full year margins to be slightly above 20%, consistent with 2025 and cash flow from operations to range between $12 billion and $13 billion.”
  • The Board authorized a new $10 billion share repurchase program and increased the quarterly dividend from $0.72 to $0.78 per share.

Financial Results

  • Revenue for the quarter grew 6.7% over the prior year period, while adjusted EBITDA increased around 11% year-over-year. Net income attributable to HCA Healthcare rose nearly 31%; diluted earnings per share as adjusted increased 29%.
  • Same facility net revenue per equivalent admission increased 2.9%. For the full year 2025, consolidated adjusted EBITDA increased 12.1% over the prior year, with a 90 basis point improvement in margin.
  • Capital expenditures reached $1.5 billion in the quarter and $4.9 billion for the year. Cash flow from operations was $12.6 billion for the year, representing a 20% increase over 2024.
  • Share repurchases for the year totaled $10 billion; dividends paid reached $679 million.

Q&A

  • Albert Rice, UBS: Asked about margin improvement opportunities in 2026 and cost assumptions. Marks responded, “The midpoint of our revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance range suggests expectations for pretty stable margins in '26 versus '25,” mentioning “continued physician cost pressures” potentially in the “high single digits growth.” When asked about contract labor, Marks confirmed, “contract labor as a percent of SWB, we came in at about 4.2% for the fourth quarter. And that feels like kind of our run rate now as we come into 2026.”
  • Ann Hynes, Mizuho: Inquired about resiliency programs and timing on exchange headwinds. Marks outlined four key focus areas for resiliency, leveraging analytics, digital transformation, and shared service platforms. He stated, “We have confidence that we'll be able to execute on this $400 million of incremental cost savings in '26 versus '25.”
  • Pito Chickering, Deutsche Bank: Asked about one-timers in 2025 and the core growth rate for 2026. Marks reiterated the $600 million to $900 million exchange impact, $250 million to $450 million decline in supplemental payments, and $400 million resiliency offset. When asked to size potential approvals, Marks said, “we're not sizing the potential approvals until we get a sense of the actual approval from CMS.”
  • Benjamin Mayo, Leerink Partners: Questioned digital initiatives with payers and working capital improvement. Marks discussed digital integration, administrative simplification, and reduction in net days in AR, attributing improvements to “sharing data and exchanging data from payers digitally.”
  • Ryan Langston, TD Cowen: Asked about M&A and capital priorities. Hazen said, “We have seen some acceleration in the outpatient space in our pipeline through acquisitions,” while noting limited opportunities in hospitals.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly cautious, with recurring questions about cost pressures, exchange headwinds, and supplemental payments. There was a focus on the sustainability of margins and visibility on resilience programs.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, highlighting consistent performance and resilience. Marks frequently emphasized confidence, stating, “we are comfortable that we will perform within our long-term plan over time.” In Q&A, management remained measured, providing detailed responses but acknowledging uncertainties, especially around supplemental payments and exchange impacts.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone remained confident but more focused on navigating policy-related headwinds, with increased detail on mitigation strategies.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is more specific, with explicit ranges for revenue, EBITDA, and net income. This is a shift from the preliminary outlook in Q3, which was more tentative due to policy uncertainty.
  • Management maintained their focus on resiliency and cost control, but the discussion of exchange-related headwinds and supplemental payment variability was more pronounced this quarter.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from general performance to detailed questions on policy headwinds, margin sustainability, and the specifics of resiliency initiatives.
  • Key metrics such as admissions growth, margin improvement, and capital allocation showed continued strength, while supplemental payment volatility and exchange impacts were highlighted as bigger variables for 2026.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified policy risks, especially the expiration of enhanced premium tax credits, Medicaid supplemental payment variability, and the uncertain rollout of Rural Health Transformation programs.
  • Marks noted, “Medicaid supplemental payment programs are complex, variable in timing and do not fully cover our cost to treat Medicaid patients.”
  • Exchange headwinds are expected to have a negative impact on adjusted EBITDA, partially offset by resiliency initiatives. Management is monitoring potential declines in health insurance exchange volumes and associated revenue impacts.
  • The paused Texas Medicaid supplemental payment program (ATLIS) was cited as contributing to a $250 million to $450 million expected decline in net benefit.
  • Analysts raised concerns about payer mix shifts, bad debt, and uncompensated care resulting from insurance changes.

Final Takeaway

HCA Healthcare’s fourth quarter and full-year 2025 results underscore strong operational performance, robust cash flow, and disciplined capital allocation. Management’s 2026 outlook reflects confidence in the company’s ability to navigate policy headwinds through targeted resiliency initiatives, continued network investment, and a substantial new share repurchase program. The focus on technology, digital transformation, and operational efficiency positions the company to sustain its long-term growth and margin targets, even as it adapts to evolving reimbursement and regulatory environments.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Space Asset Acquisition prices $200M initial public offering
data.description:
• Space Asset Acquisition (SAAQU) on Tuesday said it priced its initial public offering at $10 per unit for 20M units, with trading expected to begin Jan. 28, 2026, with the underwriter granted a 45-day option to purchase up to an additional 3M units at IPO price to cover over-allotments.
  • Each unit includes one Class A share and one-third of a redeemable warrant, with each full warrant exercisable for one share at $11.50.
  • Only whole warrants will be exercisable. Once separated, Class A shares and warrants are expected to trade on Nasdaq under the symbols SAAQ and SAAQW, respectively.
  • The offering is expected to close on January 29, 2026.

data.title: Top 3 things to look out for in the markets on Wednesday
data.description:
The U.S. stock market closed mixed on Tuesday, but the S&P 500 (SP500) achieved a new all-time high close, barely surpassing the previous, up +0.4%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) ended +0.9%, and the Dow (DJI) was -0.8%.

Now, here are the top three things to look forward to on Wednesday:

  • The Federal Reserve is expected to announce its decision on interest rate cuts on Wednesday after its two-day meeting that began on Tuesday. Odds of rates staying at the current rate of 3.5%-3.75% are at 97.2%, according to the CME FedWatch Tool.
  • The prices of precious metals, including gold (XAUUSD:CUR) and silver (XAGUSD:CUR), have continued to climb, reaching records. Gold has now surpassed the $5,180 mark and silver is now over $111. The rally is expected to continue at the same time that U.S. indexes are making new highs.
  • Ninety companies are expected to report earnings on Wednesday, including three of the Magnificent Seven—Microsoft (MSFT), Meta Platforms (META), and Tesla (TSLA)—and other giants like ASML Holding (ASML), International Business Machines (IBM), and ServiceNow (NOW).

data.title: Western Alliance outlines $6B loan and $8B deposit growth targets for 2026 amid strong fee momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Western Alliance Bancorporation (WAL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Kenneth Vecchione, CEO, highlighted that Western Alliance "closed 2025 with strong momentum delivering record quarterly financial results and broad-based performance across the franchise." He pointed to robust loan growth, reduced seasonal deposit outflows, positive net interest income trends, stable NIM, rising fee income, and continued expansion in pre-provision net revenue (PPNR), all while maintaining steady asset quality and operating leverage.
  • Vecchione announced quarterly EPS of $2.59, a 33% increase from the prior year, return on average assets at 1.23%, return on average tangible common equity at 16.9%, and tangible book value per share rising 17% year-over-year to $61.29. For the full year, the company delivered $5 billion in HFI loan growth and $10.8 billion in deposit growth.
  • Fee income was a major theme, with a 77% increase in service charges and fees for 2025, and a standout quarter for the Juris banking team, which completed over $17 million in digital payments tied to the Facebook, Cambridge Analytica settlement. Vecchione stated, "We are constructive on this business heading into 2026," citing policy tailwinds and expectations for a stronger mortgage business environment.
  • Operating leverage was emphasized, with net revenue growth outpacing noninterest expense growth by 4x, and investments made for large financial institution status. Vecchione noted, "We expect to cross $100 billion in assets by year-end 2026 without a notable step-up in expenses."
  • Vishal Idnani, CFO, reported, "In 2025, Western Alliance produced record net interest income of $2.9 billion, net revenue of $3.5 billion and pre-provision net revenue of $1.4 billion. Net income available to common shareholders was $956 million and EPS was $8.73."
  • Idnani added that noninterest income rose 25%, driven by commercial banking and disbursement fees, while noninterest expense growth slowed to 4%, aided by lower deposit and insurance costs.

Outlook

  • Vecchione detailed the 2026 outlook: "We expect loan growth of $6 billion and deposit growth of $8 billion."
  • Net interest income is projected to grow 11% to 14%, assuming two 25 basis point rate cuts. Management expects "modest expansion in net interest margin throughout the year driven by ongoing remixing into higher-return C&I categories and sustained momentum in core deposit growth."
  • Non-interest income is anticipated to grow between 2% and 4%. Total operating expenses are expected to increase between 2% and 7% for the year, with deposit costs projected to decline to between $535 million and $585 million. Operating expenses, excluding deposit costs, are guided to $1.62 billion to $1.67 billion.
  • Net charge-offs are expected between 25 and 35 basis points, with improvement in nonaccrual balances by midyear. The 2026 effective tax rate is projected at approximately 19%.

Financial Results

  • Record net interest income of $766 million was reported for Q4, up $16 million from Q3. Noninterest income rose 14% from Q3, reaching approximately $215 million. Pre-provision net revenue for Q4 was $429 million.
  • Provision expense for Q4 was $73 million, a $7 million decrease from Q3. HFI loans grew $2 billion in Q4 and total deposits were essentially flat for the quarter. For the full year, deposits rose $10.8 billion.
  • Tangible book value per share increased by $2.73 from Q3 to $61.29. The tangible common equity to tangible assets ratio rose 20 basis points to 7.3%. CET1 ratio ended the year at 11%. The company repurchased 0.7 million shares during Q4 for $57.5 million.

Q&A

  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens: Questioned conservatism in loan and deposit growth guidance. Kenneth Vecchione answered, "Our loan growth and deposit growth as projected is -- leads the peer group ... we are deemphasizing certain areas ... mostly we're doing less in residential loan growth."
  • Terrell followed up on timing of charge-offs. Vecchione responded, "You could see it a little bit higher than [midpoint] in the first half of the year as we look to get rid of a number of nonaccrual loans ... but we have a confident level that by the end of Q2, the nonaccrual loans will be down."
  • Christopher McGratty, KBW: Asked about sustainability of service charge growth and mortgage expectations. Vishal Idnani cited treasury management and digital disbursements as drivers, while Vecchione predicted a "10% year-over-year increase in total mortgage fee-related revenues."
  • David Smith, Truist: Probed on ECR deposit expectations and spreads. Idnani said, "ECR deposits to our total deposits today ... around 37% today ... I think you can assume the mix is going to move pretty consistent with where we are today."
  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: Asked about expense flexibility. Vecchione stated, "We have room there ... The secret to our success and the secret to our growth is that we always work on new businesses or new products and services, new business lines so that we can develop an S-curve."
  • Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen: Sought composition of ECR deposits. Vecchione declined specifics, citing competition, but ranked warehouse lending as largest, followed by HOA and Juris.
  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA: Asked about credit outlook. Vecchione said, "Asset quality remains stable, and there have been several notable areas of improvement."
  • Gary Tenner, D.A. Davidson: Clarified expense growth flexibility. Vecchione confirmed, "Yes."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for more detail on deposit growth, credit quality, expense flexibility, and sustainability of fee income, with a neutral to slightly positive tone but repeatedly sought clarity on risk factors and balance sheet drivers.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone, especially regarding organic growth, operating leverage, and asset quality. Vecchione often referenced leadership among peers and cited "strong loan pipelines" and "confidence in continued robust EPS growth."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone during prepared remarks remained confident, while Q&A exchanges were more measured, providing detailed explanations and occasionally declining to provide granular data due to competitive reasons. Analyst tone was consistent with prior quarter, pressing for details on emerging risks and guidance assumptions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company raised its full-year 2025 deposit growth expectations in the previous quarter, but the new 2026 guidance focuses on $6 billion loan and $8 billion deposit growth, maintaining a focus on organic expansion and specialty lines.
  • Management’s tone remained confident, but with increased emphasis on risk controls and resolution of nonaccrual loans. Fee income momentum and the digital disbursement platform received more attention this quarter.
  • Analysts continued to focus on credit quality, deposit mix, expense management, and the sustainability of fee and mortgage banking income, with recurring questions around asset quality and competitive positioning.
  • Key metrics such as EPS, tangible book value, and capital ratios all advanced, with operating leverage outpacing expense growth and a continued narrative of balance sheet strength.
  • Strategic focus shifted further toward higher-return C&I growth and away from residential loans, with a clear intention to cross the $100 billion asset threshold without a notable step-up in expenses.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged that net charge-offs are expected to remain elevated in the first half of 2026 as the company works through nonaccrual loans.
  • Asset quality remained steady, but management is proactively resolving nonaccrual balances, with improvement expected by end of Q2.
  • Analyst concerns centered on the potential for higher ECR deposit costs if rate cuts are fewer than expected, sustainability of fee income after large settlements, and the impact of competition on spreads and deposit mix.
  • Management cited ongoing legal action regarding Cantor and the expected appraisal timeline, and noted accelerated paydowns on the Point Bonita loan.

Final Takeaway

Western Alliance Bancorporation management emphasized strong organic growth, record financial results, and a constructive outlook for 2026, projecting $6 billion in loan growth and $8 billion in deposit growth, with continued expansion in higher-return C&I lending and digital fee businesses. While asset quality remains a focus, management expressed confidence in proactive risk management and operating leverage, supporting robust EPS growth in the year ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Chevron seeks improved terms from Iraq before taking over Lukoil oilfield - Reuters
data.description:
Chevron (CVX) is seeking better returns from Iraq on the giant West Qurna 2 oilfield as a condition for buying the project from Russia's Lukoil, and it is in talks with the Iraqi oil ministry to improve the contractual terms, Reuters reported Tuesday.

The Iraqi government recently nationalized the field after the U.S. imposed sanctions on Lukoil to put pressure on Russia to end its war in Ukraine; Lukoil has until February 28 to sell its assets under the U.S. sanctions.

A deal on West Qurna 2 would mark a further push into Iraq for Chevron (CVX) after the company agreed to develop several fields in the country as part of an international expansion since completing a deal to acquire Hess for $53B in 2025.

Iraq has improved the terms of its oil contracts in deals signed with global oil majors in the past two years, switching to profit-sharing agreements and away from service contracts, as it seeks to attract investment and increase production.

Lukoil's West Qurna 2 project was under the earlier service contract agreements and is said to pay among the smallest returns among all Iraqi contracts.

data.title: StandardAero sees FY2025 revenue of about $6.05B to $6.08B
data.description:
• StandardAero (NYSE:SARO) said on Tuesday that it expects FY2025 revenue of $6.05B to $6.08B, up 15.6%–16.1% from $5.24B a year earlier.
  • The company estimates FY2025 net income of $270M to $280M, compared with $11M in FY24.
  • Adjusted EBITDA is projected at $806M to $812 M, up 16.7%–17.6% from $690.5M a year ago.
  • Cash flow from operations is estimated at $310 M to $320M for FY2025, up $233.7M to $243.7M from $76.3M in FY2024.
  • Free cash flow is expected at $200M to $210M, compared with a cash use of $45M a year ago, representing an increase of $245M to $255M.
  • Consensus for FY2025 revenues is $6.01B.
  • Shares +1.22%.

data.title: Rayonier, PotlatchDeltic shareholders approve merger
data.description:
Rayonier (RYN) and PotlatchDeltic (PCH) announced that shareholders approved their merger.

The deal is expected to be completed on Friday, according to a  statement on Tuesday. The combined company will retain the Rayonier name and will trade on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker symbol "RYN." The combined company plans to announce a new name and ticker symbol later in Q1. 

PotlatchDeltic (PCH) and Rayonier (RYN) announced in October they will merge in an all-stock deal valued at ~$8.2 billion including debt, creating one of North America's largest publicly-traded timber and wood products companies.

Upon closing of the transaction, Rayonier (RYN) shareholders will own ~54% and PotlatchDeltic (PCH) shareholders will own ~46% of the combined company.
